entities.description: The principal surviving civic register of the York pageants, copied chiefly c. 1463-1477 and repeatedly revised into the sixteenth century. It contains the pageant texts represented by the encoding, lacunae and blank entries, and later additions; it is not itself the 1993 electronic text.

holding.notes: Current physical consultation is restricted and requires a letter of introduction and advance request.

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 35290 (York Register)

physicalDescription.extent: 254 folios plus starred inserts and flyleaves; the facsimile/critical-edition foliation counts 268 parchment leaves including blanks
notes: Current catalog languages are Middle English and Latin.
Pageant text is physically nonsequential at the Purification, later Coronation fragment, and several blank or inserted leaves.

rights.basis: The anonymous dramatic texts and the surviving Register long predate modern copyright terms; this statement does not cover modern editions, markup, catalog prose, or manuscript photography.

rights.basis: No public image sequence or image-specific open license was available. The British Library directs reproduction requests through Imaging Services and separate permissions procedures.

next_steps: Consult the complete 1982 edition and Kinneavy's full correction list, then collate the Register folios, OTA records, and XML serialization without treating alternative branches as additive intellectual lines.
notes: The fifty-container electronic structure and the two nested Masons/Goldsmiths pageant units are secure; the unresolved issue is internal branch genealogy.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual genealogy and editorial rationale underlie the Yk.16, Yk.16M, and Yk.16G shared and alternative blocks serialized inside Play XVI?

next_steps: Inspect the complete 1982 introduction, textual notes, and apparatus before adding any Beadle-to-Sykes or Beadle-to-HM 1 source-use relation.
notes: The currently accessible Beadle title, copyright, and contents previews do not prove either relation. Smith's explicit Sykes and Towneley comparisons are modeled separately.
openQuestions.question: Did Beadle's 1982 edition directly consult or collate the Sykes manuscript or Huntington HM 1?

next_steps: Inspect a physical or licensed complete copy of Kinneavy 1986 and record each corrected locus against the OTA files and XML.
openQuestions.question: Which exact electronic passages were changed from Beadle 1982 using Kinneavy's correction list on pp. xxxi-xxxii?

next_steps: Ask the Borthwick Institute and York City Archives to reconcile REED's current E 20:1327-1547 attribution with older York City Library and York City Archives designations.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current catalog record and shelfmark mapping for the A/Y Memorandum Book containing the Ordo Paginarum?

next_steps: Periodically recheck the exact British Library record and digitized-manuscript services; meanwhile use the 1983 print facsimile or request reference images from Imaging Services.
notes: The current catalog reports no available digital images despite historical references to full coverage.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete current public digital facsimile of British Library Add MS 35290 exist?

next_steps: Preserve Cawley's negative conclusion that neither surviving witness directly copies the other; undertake renewed stemmatic and paleographical analysis before asserting a more specific genealogy.
openQuestions.question: What lost exemplar or transmission path best explains the Sykes and Register versions of the Scriveners' pageant?

next_steps: Seek a provider or repository statement explaining the chain of grants; until then retain each electronic and distribution layer separately and follow the exact path-specific license for the repository artifact.
openQuestions.question: How should the current U-M NoC-US assertion, OTA Academic Use terms, and downstream CME_from_OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 grant be reconciled?

reviewNotes: The cataloging boundary is exactly the fifty DIV1 units in York.xml: 1, 2, 3A, 3B, 4-22, 22A, 23-46, 46A, and 47.
The Ironmongers' empty register entry and the Funeral of the Virgin, never entered in the Register, are excluded rather than invented as encoded plays.
Play XVI serializes Yk.16, Yk.16G, and Yk.16M shared and alternative blocks; its 539 L elements are not asserted as 539 additive intellectual lines.
Huntington HM 1 is a distinct Towneley compilation preserving York-derived adaptation or borrowing. Its selected digital object contains no exact York target text and is modeled target-work absent.
The exact CME_from_OTA path notice, not the repository root software license, governs the distributed York artifact and assigns CC BY-NC-SA 3.0.
Complete local-copy coverage always refers to the accessed source entity. It never silently implies a complete physical manuscript or unrestricted redistribution.

lineage.summary: The pinned repository XML is a copy of a 1993 University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive electronic edition made from Richard Beadle's 1982 edition, with corrections recorded by Gerald Byron Kinneavy in 1986. Beadle's edition principally transcribes British Library Add MS 35290. The separate Sykes copy of the Scriveners' play, documentary Ordo, and collateral York-related material in Huntington HM 1 are modeled from independently inspected sources without asserting unverified source use by Beadle. The XML encodes fifty divisions and deliberately omits front and back matter, illustrations, and editorial notes. Physical, textual, editorial, electronic, and repository layers are not collapsed into one object.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous York playwrights, revisers, and civic compilers
catalogSummary.date: Developed from at least the late fourteenth century and revised through the sixteenth century; final medieval performance in 1569
catalogSummary.editor: Richard Beadle (1982 immediate source edition); U-M/OTA electronic edition (1993)
catalogSummary.title: York Corpus Christi Play

catalogingSubject.label: York Corpus Christi Play
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous civic biblical dramatic cycle represented by the pinned U-M/OTA-derived York XML: fifty separately encoded pageant divisions drawn through Richard Beadle's 1982 edition. It is one historically assembled cycle or compilation, not fifty unrelated works. The fifty divisions preserve the 1-47 sequence plus parallel Cardmakers play 3B, the two-line Vintners' Cana remnant 22A, and later Coronation fragment 46A; they omit the textless Ironmongers entry and the unregistered Funeral of the Virgin. The medieval work, physical witnesses, Beadle's edition, the electronic encoding, and this repository artifact remain distinct transmission layers.

dateStatements.display: The pageant tradition developed over the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries; individual pageant dates and stages of revision vary
notes: The inherited XML value 1440 is an imprecise legacy collection-level date and is not normalized as a single composition year.

scope.description: Broad development window, not a claim of simultaneous composition

dateStatements.display: York civic records document pageant wagons in 1376/1377

scope.description: Earliest documentary evidence for the civic performance tradition, not attestation of every surviving text

dateStatements.display: The civic pageant tradition and constituent texts continued to be revised through the sixteenth century; the final medieval performance was in 1569

extent.description: One cycle-level compilation represented by fifty top-level XML containers and fifty-one counted pageant or fragment components. Forty-nine top-level containers count once; Play XVI is a zero-count editorial container with two nested counted pageant units, the Masons' Herod and Goldsmiths' Magi. The count retains the two-line 22A and 47-line 46A fragments as explicit components. The XML has 13,638 L elements, but no clean total of intellectual verse lines is asserted because numbering is incomplete, lacunae are represented as line elements, and Play XVI serializes shared text plus alternative Masons and Goldsmiths branches.

assertion.notes: The 13,638 XML L elements are a representation metric, not a clean intellectual line count: numbering is partial and Play XVI includes shared and alternative Goldsmiths/Masons blocks.

formStatements.description: Dramatic verse with speech labels, rubrics, stage directions, and limited embedded prose-like matter

genres.label: Cycle drama
notes: Cycle is used as a discovery term without implying a single authorial Urtext or an unchanging complete sequence.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, rubrics, and formulae are embedded rather than a sustained secondary-language component.
Guild names describe recorded production responsibility, not proven authorship.
The Funeral of the Virgin is not folded into XML Play XLIV: the encoded Death of the Virgin is a 196-line pageant, while the Funeral was never entered in the Register and is absent.
The related Towneley material is collateral adaptation or borrowing, not the exact York target work.

next_steps: Consult the complete 1982 edition and Kinneavy's correction list, then collate the shared and variant blocks without treating alternative branches as additive intellectual lines.
notes: The division is securely labeled XVI, Masons/Goldsmiths, Herod/Magi; the unresolved issue is internal branch genealogy, not play numbering.
openQuestions.question: What exact textual genealogy and editorial rationale underlie the Yk.16, Yk.16G, and Yk.16M shared and alternative blocks serialized inside Play XVI?

next_steps: Obtain a current Borthwick Institute or York City Archives catalog response; preserve the conflict between current REED attribution and older scholarship until then.
openQuestions.question: What is the current authoritative shelfmark and custody statement for the A/Y Memorandum Book containing the Ordo Paginarum?

next_steps: Continue documentary and archival research without inventing one manuscript entity per guild; treat the Sykes Scriveners copy as the only separately surviving script currently established.
openQuestions.question: What recoverable evidence remains for the lost pageant copies supplied by individual York crafts to the Register compilers?

notes: York is the civic performance, record-production, and compilation context; the statement does not prove one exact composition site for every pageant.

scope.description: The civic Register and pageant sequence

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous York playwrights, revisers, and civic compilers

scope.description: The cycle's medieval dramatic texts and revisions, without assigning individual guilds as authors

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous York playwrights, revisers, and civic compilers

scope.description: Historical assembly and continuing revision of the civic pageant sequence
The 1982 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship
The electronic text as a whole; Kinneavy's corrections affect selected passages whose exact locations are not itemized in the XML header

subjects.label: Passion, death, and resurrection of Jesus

summaries.value: A civic Middle English dramatic sequence spanning the Fall of the Angels, Creation and Old Testament history, Christ's birth, ministry, Passion and Resurrection, Marian conclusion, and Last Judgment. York crafts maintained and performed pageants on wagons at established stations; the surviving Register records a layered, revised sequence rather than a pristine authorial cycle.

The XML contains fifty top-level DIV1 pageants: the numbered 1-47 sequence, parallel play 3B, a two-line 22A remnant, and 46A's later Coronation fragment. It excludes the Ironmongers' textless register space and the Funeral of the Virgin, which was never entered in the Register. Play XVI serializes shared and alternative Masons/Goldsmiths blocks as 539 L elements and must not be treated as a simple 539-line intellectual play.

The XML has 13,638 L, 2,754 SP, 2,754 SPEAKER, 367 PB, and 368 EPB elements. Only 2,815 L elements carry N values. Direct comparison with the four OTA text files shows exactly 13,638 text rows after excluding control records, with 13,226 normalized exact row matches; this supports direct derivation but also exposes formatting, correction, and Play XVI ordering changes.

notes: Established discovery title for the historical civic dramatic compilation; the exact electronic boundary is recorded in the cataloging scope and alternate titles rather than conflated with this preferred intellectual title.
Exact title in the electronic header and Beadle's 1982 source edition.
Boundary label for the represented source selection, not a replacement title for the historical work.
titles.value: The York Plays (fifty-division U-M/OTA electronic representation)

work.author: Anonymous York playwrights, revisers, and civic compilers
work.dateDisplay: Developed from at least the late fourteenth century and revised through the sixteenth century; final medieval performance in 1569
work.editor: Richard Beadle (1982 immediate source edition); U-M/OTA electronic edition (1993)

subjects.label: Passion, death, and resurrection of Jesus

work.summary: A civic Middle English dramatic sequence spanning the Fall of the Angels, Creation and Old Testament history, Christ's birth, ministry, Passion and Resurrection, Marian conclusion, and Last Judgment. York crafts maintained and performed pageants on wagons at established stations; the surviving Register records a layered, revised sequence rather than a pristine authorial cycle.
